utterances: We got information yesterday directly from the chief in Ferguson that they had some information that suggested there were going to be acts taken against either members of the department or the department itself related to his continued employment.
A few hours later, James Knowles, the mayor of Ferguson, said he never asked Wilson to step down.
But now Wilson is totally off the payroll.
He's no longer an employee here of the city of Ferguson, and there was no severance agreement for the extension of any kinds of payments or benefits.
Some say that's not enough.
He should have been, at bare minimum, fired.
That's Osagyefo Sekou.
He came to Ferguson from Boston to protest, and he says there's more to be fixed in the Ferguson PD than just one officer.
His resignation brings no solace to this community in that this has never been about a bad apple, but it's been about a rotten system.
Sunday, Ferguson mayor James Knowles said he's focused on changing the way things work.
Our number one goal is to bring together our police department and our community, to bridge the gap between those who feel there is a divide between them and law enforcement.
The clip was posted to SoundCloud, a German-based company that has tried to position itself as the YouTube of audio.
It's got 175 million monthly listeners.
A lot of them are musicians and music fans sharing tracks, but SoundCloud's head of audio, Jim Colgan, says recently they are getting a lot more listens of people speaking.
From day one of the RNC's winter meeting, one thing was made clear: the party would be electing not one but two chairmen.
First they elected a new national chairman, longtime committee member Mike Duncan of Kentucky.
He succeeds Ken Mehlman as the man actually running the committee.
Then the RNC elected a general chairman, someone to speak for the party, and that job had Mel Martinez's name all over it.
Both Martinez and Duncan were handpicked by President Bush.
It's a long standing tradition that the president chooses his party's chair.
But this year, there was a bit of a wrinkle in the president's choice.
The state of Arizona has a lot of concerns about Mel Martinez's position on illegal immigration.
Arizona National Committeeman Randy Pullen got an earful from Republicans in his state.
They don't like it that Martinez supports legislation that would make it possible for some illegal immigrants to become citizens.
Obviously we had the Hagel-Martinez bill earlier, which was essentially an amnesty bill.
The general support of Senator Martinez for John McCain and Kennedy bill - I mean, for Arizona, that's just, it's a nonstarter.
North Dakota committeeman Curly Haugland also had problems with the proposed arrangement.
Prior to the meeting, he wrote to fellow members to say that creating a new general chairmanship would violate RNC rules.
He later complained about pressure and coercion from the White House and others in the party.
Oh, just people coming up and saying, I really wish you'd leave this thing alone, you know.
That kind of stuff.
But he didn't leave it alone.
Curly Haugland handed out a parliamentary opinion supporting his views.
And when it came time for the RNC to officially create the general chairmanship, he rose to object.
Everyone has before them an opinion that I caused to be placed there.
The new national chair, Mike Duncan, quickly took matters into his own hands.
Can you state your point of order, please?
The point of order is, I believe the motion is an improper motion, and as such...
Eventually Duncan ruled that Haugland's objection was not in order and proceedings continued.
Sharon Day from Florida formally nominated Martinez for the general chairmanship.
She assured committee members that he's someone who, quote, ""doesn't just talk the Republican talk but walks the Republican walk"" on issues like abortion and taxes.
Mel is not a halfhearted Republican.
He's not a wishy-washy Republican.
He is a Republican for a reason.
He's a Republican because he's seen what tyranny means.
He's seen it with his own eyes.
She was referring to the senator's early years in Cuba.
It's a topic Martinez himself talks about often and he did so again after being confirmed in a nearly unanimous vote.

The fact that a boy at age 15 can come here from another country with nothing, not even speaking the language, and stand before you today as the leader of our great party and a United States senator is a miracle that could only happen in America.
(Soundbite of applause)
Martinez promised to take the Republicans' message to a broader community, reaching out to Hispanics, African-Americans and other minorities.
And he acted on that promise right away.
I'll do a quick one in Spanish.
At a press briefing just after the vote, Martinez took questions from Spanish-language media and proceeded to translate them into English.
Am I going to impose my ideas on the Republican National Committee on immigration?
(Speaking Spanish)
The answer from the new voice of the Republican Party was no.
Andrea Hsu, NPR News.
Washington.
We're collecting sound clips from listeners around the country.
These are sounds people find interesting or evocative.
Today's comes from far out in the Pacific, in a house away from man-made noise.
My name is John Week, and I live in the small town of Waimea on the island of Hawaii.
Several years ago, I was experimenting with a new microphone I had bought and I was recording ambient sound in the Hawaiian upland rainforest area where I was living at the time.
And one evening I heard a dog, one of the neighborhood dogs that would occasionally serenade us in the community, singing off in the distance, and so I decided to start recording.
(Soundbite of crickets)
(Soundbite of dog in background)
(Soundbite of dog in foreground)
Now that little noise you heard just now is my dog kind of muttering in the foreground.
He's in the front yard.
You can hear the drops of rain coming off my roof and landing on the big-leafed plants around my front porch.
(Soundbite of dog in foreground)
And there's Kekoa muttering again.
And now another dog's going to join in.
And now listen to what happens when my neighbor slams a door.
(Soundbite of dog barking)
And now the full chorus joins in.
(Soundbite of dogs howling)
And as quickly as it started, it was over, except for a final comment from Kekoa.
I've kept this recording because my dog Kekoa's a big, black retriever, and he doesn't photograph well.
He's 13 years old now and doesn't do much singing anymore, or much of anything else for that matter.
And so this is my memento of him.
John Week of Waimea, Hawaii.
If you've got a sound around you that has a story or evokes a strong emotion, tell us.
You can call and leave a message at 202-408-9576, and not to worry if you couldn't get to a pencil.
That number and previous sound clips are at our Web site, NPR.org.
Well, luckily, most - you know, they promised about a dozen, maybe two dozen, possibly three dozen amendments.
In the House, we actually saw 36 amendments brought forward.
Most of those were withdrawn earlier today, but seven still remain.
And so far, they would all remove the Confederate battle flag from where it is now, but they would replace it with something else.
One called for a monument to a Cherokee chief who fought alongside the Confederate Army.
Another would replace the flagpole with a display case in which the current Confederate flag would be placed.
And there were others that would replace it with a flower bed.
One that would basically replace the current flag with lesser-known Confederate banners.
And so far, all amendments have been voted down by the South Carolina House.
And the bill needs a two-thirds vote to pass and then, of course, Governor Nikki Haley's signature.
At this point, what are the chances that that will happen?
Well, let's start with the House.
Right now, it's - the short answer is it's very likely that it's going to happen.
If you go back and look at those votes on the amendments, they have passed, you know - let me rephrase that.
The votes on the proposed amendments haven't even been close.
And opposition seems to be vocal, but small.
So that, though, may change during the actual vote on the actual bill, which will happen sometime later today early.
Earlier, the Charleston Post and Courier had been polling lawmakers, and they found the House had just enough votes to pass the measure.
But since that time, a group calling itself the Conservative Response Team has launched a robocall campaign targeting certain representatives that they see could be a swing vote on the issue.
So we'll have to see if that or anything else has an effect on the actual vote.
Now, as to Haley, Governor Haley has claimed that she would sign the bill if it comes to her without any amendments.
